coredata.dc:title: High-performance non-enzymatic glucose sensing on nanocomposite electrocatalysts of nickel phthalocyanine nanorods and nitrogen doped-reduced graphene oxide nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Adeniyi, Omotayo
Nwahara, Nnamdi
Mwanza, Daniel
Nyokong, Tebello
Mashazi, Philani
coredata.dc:description: Highly stable and efficient electrocatalytic materials are essential for developing high-performance non-enzymatic fourth-generation glucose biosensors. In this regard, hybrid materials incorporating metallophthalocyanines and carbon-based nanostructures are particularly interesting. A nanocomposite of nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide nanosheets and nickel phthalocyanine nanorods (N-rGONs|NiPcNRs) was prepared. N-rGONs|NiPcNRs were highly efficient and stable electrocatalysts for non-enzymatic d-glucose electrooxidation in alkaline media. Spectroscopic, microscopic, and electrochemical methods were used to characterize and evaluate the electrocatalytic performance of the N-rGONs|NiPcNRs. The remarkable electrocatalytic activity of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs originated from the in-situ transformation and formation of polymeric O-Ni-O oxo-bridge in alkaline electrolyte and the excellent electroconductivity of the N-rGONs. The N-rGONs|NiPcNRs with the optimum ratio of NiPcNRs to N-rGONs presented an efficient formation of the Ni-O-Ni oxo-bridge and d-glucose electrooxidation compared to N-rGONs and NiPcNRs alone. Thus, the fabricated GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs glucose sensor showed an excellent analytical performance with a high sensitivity of 1458 µA.mM−1.cm−2 and a detection limit of 1.43 µM (S/N = 3) using cyclic voltammetry. For online glucose detection using chronoamperometry, a sensitivity of 358.9 µA.mM−1.cm−2 with a detection limit of 5.0 µM (the lowest concentration that could be discriminated from the buffer using GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs) and a fast response time of 0.80 s was obtained. The high sensitivity of CV (1458 µA.mM−1.cm−2) allowed for lower limits of detection (1.34 µM). The fabricated sensor was successfully applied for the determination of d-glucose levels in serum samples, demonstrating its potential for monitoring glucose levels in diabetes patients.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs in 0.50 M KOH containing different d-glucose concentrations from 0.0 to 20 mM at a scan rate of 50 mV.s−1 and the (b) corresponding calibration curve. (c) Amperometric current vs time (i-t) response of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs in 0.50 M KOH with successive addition of varying d-glucose concentrations from 5.0 µM to 6.0 mM and (d) corresponding calibration curves.

Amperometric response of GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs to successive addition of 0.25 mM of d-glucose, 0.50 mM of other interfering substances, and 0.10 M NaCl, (b) repeated addition of 0.25 mM of d-glucose at 30 s interval in 0.50 M KOH and (c) bar graph representing the CV current response to 0.50 mM d-glucose measured every-five days within 40 days.
Comparison of the analytical performance of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs to other reported glucose sensors. (TW – this work), aCV and bAmperommetry.

head.ce:title: High-performance non-enzymatic glucose sensing on nanocomposite electrocatalysts of nickel phthalocyanine nanorods and nitrogen doped-reduced graphene oxide nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa (P. Mashazi).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nanocomposite of NiPc nanorods with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots was prepared and drop casted onto the glassy carbon electrode to form GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs. The nanocomposite electrode was converted into polymeric -[O-NiPc-O-NiPc]n- in alkaline media to form nanocatalysts for selective non-enzymatic electrooxidation of d-glucose.

Highly stable and efficient electrocatalytic materials are essential for developing high-performance non-enzymatic fourth-generation glucose biosensors. In this regard, hybrid materials incorporating metallophthalocyanines and carbon-based nanostructures are particularly interesting. A nanocomposite of nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide nanosheets and nickel phthalocyanine nanorods (N-rGONs|NiPcNRs) was prepared. N-rGONs|NiPcNRs were highly efficient and stable electrocatalysts for non-enzymatic d-glucose electrooxidation in alkaline media. Spectroscopic, microscopic, and electrochemical methods were used to characterize and evaluate the electrocatalytic performance of the N-rGONs|NiPcNRs. The remarkable electrocatalytic activity of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs originated from the in-situ transformation and formation of polymeric O-Ni-O oxo-bridge in alkaline electrolyte and the excellent electroconductivity of the N-rGONs. The N-rGONs|NiPcNRs with the optimum ratio of NiPcNRs to N-rGONs presented an efficient formation of the Ni-O-Ni oxo-bridge and d-glucose electrooxidation compared to N-rGONs and NiPcNRs alone. Thus, the fabricated GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs glucose sensor showed an excellent analytical performance with a high sensitivity of 1458 µA.mM−1.cm−2 and a detection limit of 1.43 µM (S/N = 3) using cyclic voltammetry. For online glucose detection using chronoamperometry, a sensitivity of 358.9 µA.mM−1.cm−2 with a detection limit of 5.0 µM (the lowest concentration that could be discriminated from the buffer using GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs) and a fast response time of 0.80 s was obtained. The high sensitivity of CV (1458 µA.mM−1.cm−2) allowed for lower limits of detection (1.34 µM). The fabricated sensor was successfully applied for the determination of d-glucose levels in serum samples, demonstrating its potential for monitoring glucose levels in diabetes patients.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Realising accurate determination of glucose is essential in food processing industries and medical diagnosis, including clinical management of diabetes. Since Clark and Lyons invented the first enzyme electrode in 1962 [1], glucose oxidase (GOx) has been utilized to develop commercial glucose biosensors. GOx selectively catalyzes glucose oxidation using molecular oxygen as an electron acceptor to form gluconolactone and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Over the years, glucose biosensors have evolved to achieve low-cost, accurate, and enhanced glucose sensing performance through various technological advancements. The glucose levels in the first-generation sensor were quantified by the current response from the oxidation of generated H2O2 or oxygen consumed at a platinum electrode [2,3]. GOx was conjugated to redox-active materials to mediate electron transfer from the solution phase to the electrode surface in the 2nd generation glucose sensor [4]. In the 3rd generation glucose sensor, GOx was wired to nanomaterials to allow direct electron transfer between GOx and the electrode surface [5–7]. Despite these advances, the enzymatic glucose sensor suffers from inherent limitations of the GOx enzyme. These limitations included (i) the persistent poor electron transfer, (ii) the dependence of GOx activity on oxygen concentrations, (iii) difficulties in GOx purification, (iv) poor temperature stability, and (v) susceptibility to pH and humidity changes [3,8]. These limitations have significantly impacted the accuracy and reliability of enzymatic glucose biosensors [9]. Therefore, developing a fourth-generation glucose sensor based on non-enzymatic glucose electrooxidation is an effective alternative to overcome the limitations of the enzymatic GOx-based glucose biosensors.

To date, noble metal nanoparticles (AuNPs [10–12], PdNPs [13], PtNPs [14]), transition metal nanoparticles (CuNPs [15,16], CoNPs [17–19], and NiNPs [20–24]) and bimetallic nanoparticles (Cu/CoNPs [25], Co/FeNPs [26], NiFeNPs [27], and Au@NiONPs [28]) have been reported for non-enzymatic electrochemical glucose sensing. Among these, nickel-based nanomaterials have shown efficient glucose-sensing capabilities due to their highly catalytic Ni2+/Ni3+ redox couple in alkaline media [24,29]. However, the lack of well-defined nanostructure and quantifiable active sites in nickel oxide nanocatalysts often leads to poor sensor reproducibility [24]. Also, the low electronic conductivity, low ion diffusion rate, poor air, and thermal stability have limited their applications. Stable nickel-based electrocatalytic materials are required for developing a non-enzymatic fourth-generation electrochemical glucose sensor.

Nickel phthalocyanine (NiPc) is a transition metal complex with an 18 π-electron macrocyclic framework containing four isoindole moieties creating a cavity for inserting metal ions such as nickel (II). NiPc has outstanding advantages such as well-defined molecular structure, quantifiable redox-active sites, cost-effective preparation, inherent air, and chemical stability [30,31]. The one-dimensional (1D) nanostructure of NiPc has attracted significant interest due to its large surface area [32,33]. These features make 1D NiPc nanorod a promising electrocatalyst for high-performance non-enzymatic glucose sensing. However, the poor electroconductivity of NiPc limits the electrocatalytic activity [34,35]. The integration of NiPc with two-dimensional highly conductive graphene oxide nanosheets (GONs) improves the electrical conductivity and electrolyte transport properties of NiPcNRs [36,37]. Reduced graphene oxide nanosheets (rGONs) are two-dimensional (2D) highly conjugated carbon networks with remarkable properties such as high surface area, high mechanical strength, and efficient electroconductivity [38,39]. When doped with nitrogen (N) to form N-rGONs, the N promotes charge carrier mobility and wettability of rGONs [40,41]. N-rGONs possess high electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic activity [42]. Considering these advantages, integrating N-rGONs with NiPcNRs could provide hybrid material with synergistic properties.

In this work, a hybrid of nickel phthalocyanine nanorods-nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide nanosheets (N-rGONs|NiPcNRs) was prepared and evaluated for electrocatalytic detection of glucose. Spectroscopic, microscopic, and electrocatalytic properties of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs were investigated. The NiPcNRs and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs deposited on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) were electrochemically transformed to a high valent Ni-oxo-bridged form in an alkali solution. The effect of the mass ratio of N-rGONs to NiPcNRs on the formation of Ni-oxo-bridge and its catalytic activity towards glucose oxidation was evaluated. The GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs were used to quantify glucose in serum samples to demonstrate clinical application. The novelty in this work includes (i) facile preparation of 1D nanostructured NiPcNRs and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs nanocomposite material, (ii) demonstrating the electrochemical transformation of NiPcNRs and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs to highly electrocatalytic polymeric Ni-O-Ni oxo-bridges and (iii) fabrication of a low-cost, highly stable, and ultrasensitive fourth-generation non-enzymatic glucose sensor based on direct electrocatalytic oxidation of glucose at the GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs electrode.

All the reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and were used as received from the suppliers without further purification. Graphite flakes (99 %, 50 mesh size); potassium permanganate (KMnO4); sodium nitrate (NaNO3); nickel acetate tetrahydrate Ni(CH3CO2)2·4H2O; 1,2-dicyanobenzene; 1,8-diazabicyclo[5,4,0]undec-7-ene (DBU); d-glucose; sucrose (Suc); fructose (Fru); urea (Ur), Uric Acid (UA); dopamine (DA) and ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (South Africa). Potassium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium chloride (KCl), dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol, sulphuric acid (H2SO4), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Merck Chemicals (South Africa). Highly purified water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.cm was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore Corp, Bedford, USA) and used to prepare all aqueous solutions throughout the experiment.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out on an AUTOLAB PGSTAT302N Potentiostat/Galvanostat equipped with NOVA 1.10 software and FRA32M for impedance measurement and analysis. The electrochemical cell consists of three electrodes: a silver-silver chloride (Ag|AgCl) reference electrode, a platinum wire counter electrode, and a bare GCE or modified GCE working electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and chronoamperometry measurements were carried out at room temperature. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Discover equipped with a Lynx Eye detector, using Cu-Ka radiation (1.5405 Å, nickel filter). UV–vis spectra were measured using the Thermo-Scientific, Multiskan SKY Microplate equipped with holder Cuvette and Touch Screen, Belgium. The XRD sample preparation and data analysis were reported previously [43]. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) micrographs were obtained using a Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80 kV. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and the elemental mapping were carried out using an INCA PENTA FET coupled to VAGA TESCAN using a 20-kV accelerating voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), survey, and high-resolution core-level spectra were collected using the Kratos Axis Ultra-DLD equipment. The XPS analysis and the curve fittings setup were previously described [44].

A mixture of 1,2-dicyanobenzene (0.50 g, 4.0 mmol) and Ni(CH3CO2)2·4H2O (0.50 g, 2.0 mmol) were homogenously ground and placed in a heat resistance quartz vessel. Then, 0.50 mL of DBU was added, and the resulting pasty mixture was irradiated in a microwave oven at 700 W for 20 min. After cooling to room temperature, the product was refluxed in 10 mL of ethanol for 2 hrs. The precipitate was washed with methanol, 1.0 M HCl, and mild hot water to remove the residual reagent. After drying, the product was extracted with methanol. The greenish-purple precipitate was dried in an oven at 60 °C until a constant weight of 0.40 g. The yield was 79.9 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide nanosheets (N-rGONs)
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide nanosheets (GONs) were synthesised from graphite flakes using a modified Hummers method [45]. Briefly, graphite flakes (2.0 g) were irradiated in a microwave oven at 700 W for 60 s to produce expanded graphite flakes (EG). Concentrated H2SO4 (98 %, 100 mL) was added to a mixture of EG (2.0 g) and NaNO3 (2.0 g) and stirred in an ice bath for 30 min. KMnO4 (3.5 g) was added, and the solution was slowly stirred for 4 hrs. The temperature of the reaction mixture was raised to 90 °C and left for 6 hrs. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature. Then, 100 mL of ice-cold water and 100 mL of H2O2 (30 %) were added and stirred for 2 hrs. The reaction mixture was centrifuged to obtain the graphene oxide nanosheets (GONs). The GONs were washed three times with 100 mL of HCl (1.0 M) to remove metal impurities and repeatedly washed with 100 mL of deionized H2O until the pH became neutral (pH 7.0). The GONs were dried at 50 °C for 24 hrs.

GONs (50 mg) were dispersed in 100 mL of DMF and sonicated at 400 W for 12 hrs until a yellow exfoliated GONs solution was obtained. Hydrazine (500 µL) was added to 10 mL of the exfoliated GONs solution (2.0 mg.mL −1) and irradiated in a microwave oven at 700 W for 20 min. The black-coloured solution was mixed with ethanol, centrifuged at 3000 rpm, and repeatedly washed with water until the pH was neutral. The obtained nitrogen-doped reduced graphene (N-rGONs) was dried at 60 °C until it had a constant weight.

N-rGONs (50 mg) were dispersed in 50 mL of DMF at a final concentration of 1.0 mg.mL −1 and sonicated for 6 hrs at 500 W to obtain exfoliated N-rGONs solution. The NiPcNRs (50 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL of DMF to obtain 5.0 mg.mL −1. N-rGONs|NiPcNRs with various ratios of N-rGONs:NiPcNRs were prepared by mixing different volumes of N-rGONs solutions (1.0 mg.mL −1) and NiPcNR solutions (5.0 mg.mL −1), sonicated overnight and irradiated in a microwave oven at 700 W for 2 min to form a purplish-green N-rGONs|NiPcNRs precipitate. The N-rGONs|NiPcNRs were centrifuged to remove DMF and dried at 70 °C for 12 hrs. The N-rGONs|NiPcNRs (2.0 mg) were dispersed in 1.0 mL of DMF and sonicated for 30 min to obtain a highly viscous greenish-purple N-rGONs|NiPcNRs ink. The prepared ratio (wt/wt) were 1:0.5 (N-rGONs1|NiPcNR0.5), 1:1 (N-rGONs1|NiPcNR1), 1:2 (N-rGONs1|NiPcNRs2), 1:5 (N-rGONs1|NiPcNRs5) and 1:10 (N-rGONs1|NiPcNRs10).

A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was cleaned by polishing sequentially with 1.0 μm, 0.30 μm, and 0.050 μm size Al2O3 slurry, and after each polish, the electrode was rinsed thoroughly with deionized H2O. The GCE was finally sonicated in a 50 % ethanol/H2O mixture to remove Al2O3 residue. The GCE was electrochemically treated by scanning in 0.50 M KOH solution between −1.0 V and 1.0 V vs Ag|AgCl. It was then washed with H2O and dried in a stream of N2 gas. The cleaned GCE was individually modified by drop-casting N-rGONs, NiPcNRs, or N-rGON|NiPcNRs solution (2.0 mg.mL −1, 10 μL) and dried at room temperature to yield GCE|N-rGONs, GCE|NiPcNRs, and GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs, respectively. The N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs forms an ink that strongly adheres to the GCE surface without using a binder. The stability of the GCE|N-rGON|NiPcNRs modified film is due to the π-π interaction between the GCE and N-rGON|NiPcNRs. In addition, cycling the electrode in 0.50 M KOH solution at 50 mV.s−1 for 20 cycles resulted in highly stable polymeric N-rGON|NiPcNRs film on the GCE.

NiPcNRs were synthesised by microwave irradiation of a mixture of nickel acetate, 1,2-dicyanobenzene, and a strong organic base. The nickel (II) ion coordinates with the cyano groups and acts as a template to promote phthalocyanine macrocyclic ring formation. The microwave heating fosters the growth of nickel phthalocyanine macrocycle to one-dimensional (1D) self-assembled nickel phthalocyanine nanorods (NiPcNRs) via π-π interaction. Compared to conventional solvothermal phthalocyanine synthetic procedures, the microwave-assisted method used in this work is rapid, simple, efficient, and environmentally friendly. N-rGONs were prepared by hydrazine-assisted simultaneous reduction and nitrogen doping of GONs. The mechanism of hydrazine reduction and simultaneous N-doping of GONs was previously reported [46,47]. The NiPcNRs were integrated with N-rGONs via π-π interaction to form N-rGONs|NiPcNRs nanocomposites. The composition and morphology of the synthesised materials were investigated using various techniques. Fig. 1 shows (a) UV–vis, (b) FT-IR, (c) XRD, and (d) Raman spectra of (i) GONs (ii) N-rGONs, (iii) NiPcNRs, and (iv) N-rGONs|NiPcNRs nanocomposites. The ground state absorption spectra of GONs and N-rGONs in DMF showed UV–vis absorption without a peak between 300 and 800 nm, as shown in Fig. 1(a)(i) and (ii). The UV–vis spectra of NiPcNRs and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs in Fig. 1(a)(iii) and (iv) showed broad B-bands (soret) due to So-S2 transition in the ultraviolet region and split Q-bands due to So-S1 transition in the visible region of the spectra. The B-bands absorptions were at 358 nm and 347 nm for NiPcNRs and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs, respectively. Compared to NiPcNRs, the blue shift and an increase in the intensity of the B-bands relative to the Q-bands of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs were observed. This confirmed the π-π interaction between the NiPcNRs and the N-rGONs. The weak absorption peak at 470 nm could be attributed to the ligand-to-metal (LMCT) or metal-to-ligand (MLCT) charge transfer band. The spilt Q-bands resulted from the exciton coupling of neighboring phthalocyanine molecules. The Q-bands at 614 nm and 688 nm were due to H-type and J-type aggregate exciton coupling. The UV–vis spectra of NiPcNRs showed the dominance of H-type aggregate, confirming the formation of nanorods. The UV–vis spectrum of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs in Fig. 1(a)(iv) showed a relative increase in the peak intensity at 688 nm, indicating π-stacking of NiPcNRs to N-rGONs to form J-type N-rGONs|NiPcNRs nanocomposites.

The FT-IR spectra of GONs in Fig. 1(b)(i) show broad bands between 3658 cm−1 and 2152 cm−1, attributed to the overlapping of OH and CH groups. The IR band at 1627 cm−1 was due to the CC stretching vibrations of the graphitic carbon structure. The GONs spectrum shows oxygen functional groups characterized by CO stretching vibrations at 1718 cm−1, CO at 1376 cm−1, CO (epoxy) stretching vibrations at 1238 cm−1, CO (alkoxy) stretching vibration at 1040 cm−1 and bending vibrations at 976 cm−1. After the hydrazine-assisted reduction via microwave irradiation, the FT-IR spectrum of N-rGONs in Fig. 1(b)(ii) showed a disappearance of the oxygen functional groups, which confirms the reduction of the GONs to form N-rGONs. The new weak band at 3745 cm−1 in Fig. 1(b)(ii) was attributed to the NH stretch vibrations, and the broadband with maxima absorption at 1576 cm−1 was attributed to the CC bending vibrations. These new bands indicate nitrogen doping and restoration of the graphitic conjugated structures in the N-rGONs. In addition, the FT-IR spectrum of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs in Fig. 1(b)(iv) had bands in the fingerprint region that corresponds to the characteristics of NiPcNRs in Fig. 1(b)(iii), confirming the integration of NiPcNRs into the N-rGONs framework. The band at 916 cm−1 and 947 cm−1 were attributed to N-Ni ligand vibrational mode [48]. The bands at 1288 cm−1 and 1333 cm−1 were due to CN-C stretching vibrations of the inner isoindole ring. The bands between 1427 cm−1 and 1611 cm−1 were attributed to the out-of-plane and in-plane CH stretching and bending vibrations of the aromatic ring [49]. Metallophthalocyanines have different polymorphs (β, α, x, y) identified by FT-IR absorption in the 700–800 cm−1 region [50]. The NiPcNRs have a sharp band at 722 cm−1, indicating a rich α-phase [51]. However, the FT-IR spectrum of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs had absorbance bands at 717, 732, 760, 800, and 865 cm−1, demonstrating the occurrence of both α- and β-phases. The incomplete transformation of α-phase NiPcNRs to its β-phase occurred during the synthesis of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs.

The crystallinity and phase transformation was investigated using an X-ray diffractogram (XRD). The XRD pattern of GONs in Fig. 1(c)(i) showed a diffraction peak at (2θ) = 11.2°, corresponding to the (001) reflection plane of GONs with an interplanar distance of 0.75 nm. After the hydrazine-assisted microwave thermal reduction, the (001) peak of the GONs disappeared. Two new broad diffraction bands at (2θ) = 25.2° and 43.5° were attributed to the (002) and (101) crystalline planes, respectively, were observed in the XRD pattern of N-rGONs in Fig. 1(c)(ii). The diffraction peak at (2θ) = 25.2° corresponds to the interlayer distance of 0.40 nm, and at 43.5° indicated the formation of a stacked nanostructure in N-rGONs. The disappearance of the (001) peak and the reduction of the interlayer distance confirm oxygenated group removal and the successful formation of N-rGONs. The XRD pattern of NiPcNRs and N-rGON|NiPcNRs showed diffraction peaks at 10.3°, 13.5°, 14.8°, 15.9°, 21.2°, 24.0°, 24.9°, 26.4°, 29.5°, and 30.1°. The diffraction patterns were indexed to the Miller indices for (110)α, (102)β, (202)α, (102)β, (104)β, (111)β, (213)β, (214)β, (106)β and (406)β. The diffraction patterns were consistent with monoclinic β-phase NiPc (JCPDS No: 11–0744), and patterns due to the α-phase were also identified. The XRD pattern confirms a mixture of β-phase and α-phase polymorphs in the N-rGONs|NiPcNRs.

The Raman spectra were recorded to identify the structural alterations. The Raman spectrum of GONs displayed a d-band at 1354 cm−1 corresponding to the graphitic step-edges and sp3 defect features. Also, a G-band at 1620 cm−1 corresponding to the in-phase vibration of sp2 carbon atoms of GONs was observed. The high intensity of the D-band indicated the presence of oxygenated groups in GONs. In the Raman spectrum of N-rGONs in Fig. 1(d)(ii), the D-band and G-band positions were at 1315 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1, respectively, and shifted to a lower frequency than the GONs. This further confirmed the reduction in the oxygen functional groups and nitrogen doping in N-rGONs. The ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) was used to indicate the degree of defect in graphitic materials. The ID/IG of GONs was 0.68 and increased to 0.89 for N-rGONs, confirming the successful reduction and doping of nitrogen into the graphitic framework. The Raman spectra of NiPcNRs exhibit several typical peaks at 706, 754, 893, 941, 1350, 1489, 1546, 1687, 2129, 2407, 2709, and 2819 cm−1, which are consistent with previous reports [52,53]. The Raman spectrum of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs exhibited signals originating from both N-rGONs and NiPcNRs. The Raman shift of the G-band decreased from 1604 cm−1 for N-rGONs to 1593 cm−1 for N-rGONs|NiPcNRs, which is due to π-π stacking interactions between N-rGONs and NiPcNRs. The ID/IG of N-rGONs and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs showed a noticeable decrease from 0.89 to 0.15. This confirmed that π-stacking interaction between N-rGONs and NiPcNRs increased the degree of sp2 characteristics of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were recorded to characterize the elemental composition and the oxidation state of the elements in the materials. Fig. 2 represents the XPS survey spectra of (i) GONs, (ii) N-rGONs, (iii) NiPcNRs, and (iv) N-rGONs|NiPcNRs. The atomic percentage (% at) of the elements and their ratio as an inset in Fig. 2. The survey spectra of GONs, N-rGONs, and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs confirmed the presence of carbon (C 1 s) and oxygen (O 1 s) at binding energies (BE) of 285 eV and 532 eV, respectively. In addition, the survey spectrum of N-rGONs in Fig. 2 (ii) showed a nitrogen (N 1 s) peak at 401 eV, confirming the nitrogen doping to form N-rGONs. The % of the nitrogen in N-rGONs was about 7.4 %, and the nitrogen-to-carbon (N/C) ratio was 0.09. The ratio of oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) decreased from 0.50 for GONs to 0.14 for N-rGONs, confirming the efficient reduction of the oxygen functionality in N-rGONs. In addition to the carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, the XPS survey spectra of NiPcNRs and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs showed the presence of Ni 2p1/2 at 855 eV and Ni 2p3/2 at 872 eV. The % atomic composition on the surface of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs was 67.8 % C 1 s, 12.2 % O 1 s, 11.9 % N 1 s, and 8.1 % Ni 2p.

The high-resolution core-level C 1 s spectrum of GONs in Fig. 3 (a) was deconvoluted into four components. The component at 284.9 eV (44.9 %) was attributed to the graphitic lattice CC, CC bonds. The components at 286.9 eV (38.5 %) were attributed to CO of the epoxy and phenolic groups, and at 288.4 eV (13.1 %) were attributed to the CO and COC of lactones, ketones, and carboxyl groups of GONs. A shake-up π-π* satellite peak of the graphitic sp2 carbons was observed at 290.8 eV (3.6 %). The microwave-assisted hydrazine reduction leads to the formation of N-rGONs. The core-level C 1 s spectrum is in Fig. 3 (a'). The subsequent integration of N-rGONs with NiPcNRs formed N-rGONs|NiPcNRs, and the C 1 s spectrum is in Fig. 3 (a''). The C 1 s spectra for N-rGONs and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs were fitted into five components at 284.9, 285.7, 287.3, 288.7, and 290.8 eV. The component at 284.9 eV was also assigned to the CC and CC bonds of N-rGONs and NiPcNRs. However, the relative intensity of the CC and CC for N-rGONs was 53.1 % and higher than 44.9 % for GONs, confirming the restoration of the sp2 graphitic lattice N-rGONs. The increase in the shake-up π-π* satellite peak of GONs from 3.6 % to 7.1 % in N-rGONs further confirms the restoration of the graphitic carbon structure in N-rGONs. The other components for CO (6.3 %) and CO (10.9 %) in N-rGONs were lower than CO (13.1 %) and CO (38.5 %) in GONs, further confirming the efficient reduction of the oxygenated groups. The new components at 285.7 eV (22.7 %) and 286.4 (9.9 %) were attributed to CN groups of pyridinic and pyrrolic carbon in N-rGONs and NiPcNRs. These components confirm the integration of N-rGONs with the NiPcNRs. The high-resolution core-level N 1 s spectrum of N-rGONs in Fig. 3 (b') showed four components at 399.4 eV, 400.7 eV, 402.3 eV, and 404.1 eV, attributed to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N, and N-oxide, respectively. The nitrogen in N-rGONs is mainly pyrrolic nitrogen (60.9 %) and pyridinic nitrogen (22.8 %), which are essential for efficient electrochemical activity [54]. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum of NiPcNRs was deconvoluted into two components at 398.9 eV and 400.8 eV attributed to pyridinic-N (CNNi) bonded to an outer carbon atom of NiPcNRs and pyrrolic-N bonded to Ni. The N 1 s spectrum of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs exhibited three components at 399.4 eV (61.2 %), 400.7 eV (32.5 %), and 401.9 eV (6.3 %) attributed to the pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N, respectively. The increase in pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs compared to N-rGONs was attributed to contributions of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N of NiPcNRs. The high-resolution Ni 2p core-level spectrum of NiPcNR in Fig. 3(c) and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs in Fig. 3 (c') showed spin–orbit coupling and shake-up satellite peaks, indicating the +2 oxidation state of Ni. The Ni 2p½ and Ni 2p3/2 in the spectra of NiPcNRs were at 857.9 eV and 875.3 eV, respectively, and 855.8 eV and 872.8 eV, respectively for N-rGONs|NiPcNRs. The binding energy shift of Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 in the N-rGONs|NiPcNRs, as referenced against NiPcNRs, were −2.0 eV and −2.5 eV, respectively. The spin–orbit splitting between the Ni 2p½ and Ni 2p3/2 was 17.4 eV and 17.0 eV for the NiPcNRs and N-rGONs|NiPcNRs, respectively. The lower binding energies and spin–orbit splitting of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs compared to the NiPcNRs strongly suggest electron transfer interactions between N-rGONs and NiPcNRs.

Fig. 4 shows (a) scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and (b) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs of (i) N-rGONs, (ii) NiPcNRs, and (iii) N-rGON|NiPcNRs. The SEM micrographs of N-rGONs showed a stacked layer of corrugated nanosheets with folds and wrinkles, in Fig. 4(a)(i). The TEM micrographs in Fig. 4(b)(i) confirmed the wrinkling effect of the N-rGONS. For the NiPcNRs, the SEM in Fig. 4(a)(ii) micrograph showed rod-like morphology and the TEM in Fig. 4(b)(ii) confirmed the rod-like structures with a diameter of 90 ± 10 nm and a length of 1.90 ± 0.70 μm and as shown in Fig. S1
(a) and (b). A highly interlinked structure of NiPcNRs sparsely embedded within and on the surface of the wrinkled N-rGONs layers was observed in the SEM micrograph of the N-rGONs|NiPcNRs in Fig. 4(a)(iii). The SEM of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs confirmed the stacking of NiPcNRs onto the N-rGONs. The TEM micrograph in Fig. 4(b)(iii) showed an increase in the diameter of rod-like morphology from 90 ± 10 nm for NiPcNRs to 200 ± 80 nm for N-rGONs|NiPcNRs in Fig. S1
(c), and the length increased from 1.90 ± 0.70 μm for NiPcNRs to 3.25 ± 1.30 μm for N-rGONs|NiPcNRs ( Fig. S1
(d). The increasing diameter and length confirm the formation of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs nanocomposite materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of GCE|NiPcNRs and GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behaviour of the fabricated electrodes in the supporting electrolyte (KOH solution) was investigated. Fig. 5 shows cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of (a) GCE|N-rGONs, (b) GCE|NiPcNRs, and (c) GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs (1:5 wt/wt) in 0.50 M KOH within a potential between −1.0 to 0.80 V obtained at a scan rate of 50 mV.s−1 for 20 scans. The CV of GCE|N-rGONs in Fig. 5(a) did not show a well-resolved faradaic redox peak, indicating that the N-rGONs do not undergo an efficient redox process within the studied potential window. The high background current of GCE|N-rGONs could be attributed to the large electroactive surface area. The CVs of GCE|NiPcNRs and GCE|N-rGON1|NiPc5NRs in 0.50 M KOH solution showed a series of faradaic redox peaks between −0.80 and 0.70 V, Fig. 5(b) and (c). The increase in the background current observed at Ep > 0.6 V could be attributed to the formation of the Ni-O-Ni oxo-bridge in the KOH electrolyte solution [55]. The coordination of the oxygen atom (O) with the central Ni metal in the phthalocyanine ring occurred during the electrochemical cycling in KOH. The anodic peaks at −0.21 V and 0.34 V in the CV of GCE|NiPcNRs were ascribed to the irreversible transformation of NiPcNRs to Pc-Ni-O-Ni-Pc [56,57]. The reversible redox couple at 0.42 V and 0.55 V were attributed to the Ni2 +/Ni3 + redox transformation, while the irreversible cathodic peak at −0.52 V was due to the metallophthalocyanine ring reduction process [57]. On continuous cycling in the KOH electrolyte solution, the anodic and cathodic peak current magnitude increases with increasing CV scans until a steady state is reached after the 20th scan. The increase in the peak currents with an increasing number of scans confirms the transformation of the NiPcNRs into interconnected polymeric Ni-O-Ni. Similar catalytic oxidation and reduction peaks were observed in Fig. 5(c) for GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs with enhanced and more resolved peaks. This shows that N-rGONs enhances the electrocatalytic activity of NiPcNRs. Nitrogen doped GONs have electrons accepting nitrogen sites and these improve the electron transfer efficiency with NiPcNR molecules. The redox-active sites of NiPcNRs, especially the oxo-bridged species resulted in the polymeric species also contributing to the conducting properties of the composite material. Combining these materials resulted in a synergistic effect towards high catalytic and conducting effect and far better than the bare GCE and GCE|NiPcNRs.

The effect of the weight ratio of NiPcNRs to N-rGONs on the behaviour of N-rGONs|NiPcNRs in KOH solution was studied. The weight ratio of N-rGONs to NiPcNRs studied and their abbreviation shown in brackets are (wt/wt): 1:0.5 (N-rGONs1|NiPc0.5NRs), 1:1 (N-rGONs1|NiPc1NRs), 1:2 (N-rGONs1|NiPc2NRs), 1:5 (N-rGONs|NiPc5NRs) and 1:10 (N-rGONs1|NiPc10NRs). Fig. S2 represent the CVs of (i) GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc0.5NRs, (ii) GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc1NRs, (iii) GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc2NRs and (iv) GCE|N-rGONs1 |NiPc10NRs in 0.50 mol.L-1 KOH. The CV behaviour varied with changes in the ratio of N-rGONs to NiPcNRs. The GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc0.5NRs, GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc1NRs, and GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc2NRs at a ratio of < 1:5 wt/wt in Fig. S2
(a)-(c) showed a progressive decrease in the anodic peak current at 0.58 V, while the reduction peak at 0.40 V had a small increase with an increasing number of CV scans. This suggested that the Ni2+/Ni3+ redox transformation was not efficient at a ratio of < 1:5 wt/wt. It could be inferred that NiPcNRs were monomerically well-dispersed onto the N-rGONs at a ratio of N-rGONs to NiPcNRs < 1:5, preventing the formation of polymeric Ni-O-Ni oxo bridges. However, at an N-rGONs to NiPcNRs ratio of ≥ 1:5, the CV of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs in Fig. 5(c) and GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc10NRs in Fig. S2
(d) showed a progressive increase in the magnitude of the anodic and cathodic peak current with an increasing number of CV scans. At a higher wt/wt ratio of ≥ 1:5, the formation of NiPcNRs aggregates promotes the formation of the Ni-O-Ni oxo-bridges, confirming the efficient formation of electrocatalytic Ni2 +/Ni3 + species. The current magnitude of GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs was substantially higher than the GCE|NiPcNRs in Fig. 5(b), indicating that N-rGONs significantly enhanced the electrochemical activity of the NiPcNRs.

Furthermore, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using [FeCN6]3-/4- redox probe was used to study the electron transport properties of the modified electrodes. Fig. 6 shows (a) CVs and (b) Nyquist plots of (i) GCE, (ii) GCE|N-rGONs, (iii) GCE|NiPcNRs, and (iv) GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs in 2.0 mM of [FeCN6]3-/4- containing 0.10 M KCl. The [FeCN6]3-/4- exhibits reversible redox behaviour at the bare GCE electrode with a peak-to-peak separation (ΔE) of 85 ± 11 mV and a peak current ratio of 0.92. The ΔE increased to 206 ± 33 mV for GCE|NiPcNRs, indicating that NiPcNRs inhibit the [FeCN6]3-/4- redox process. The CVs of GCE|N-rGONs and GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs showed a higher current response and an ΔE value of 98 ± 5 mV and 100 ± 7 mV, respectively. The increased current ratio and lowered ΔE value of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs compared with GCE|NiPcNRs (206 ± 33 mV) indicated an increase in the electroactive surface area and enhanced electroconductivity of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs. The formal potential of [FeCN6]3-/4- was 0.26 V and was applied for the impedance measurement. The impedance data was fitted with a Randles-Sevcik equivalent circuit with the topology previously reported [44]. The fitted circuit parameters were accepted only when the % error was < 5.0 %. The magnitude of the fitted data was summarised in Table S1 . The Nyquist plot of the bare GCE in Fig. 6(b) showed a small semi-circle corresponding to a charge-transfer resistance (RCT) of 0.36 ± 0.05 kΩ and capacitance of 1.49 ± 0.23 µF. The RCT and capacitance increased to 1.67 ± 0.9 kΩ and 2.7 ± 0.4 µF, respectively, for the GCE|NiPcNRs. The increase in the RCT of the GCE|NiPcNRs relative to the bare GCE was due to the poor conductivity and low ion permeability of NiPcNRs. The Nyquist plot of GCE|N-rGONs and GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs showed a straight line, and there was no observable semi-circle at the high frequency, which indicates an enhanced charge transfer process. The capacitance of the GCE|N-rGONs, and GCE|N-rGON1|NiPc5NRs were significantly higher than that of the GCE and GCE|NiPcNRs. The capacitance of the GCE|N-rGONs was 1320 ± 62 µF and decreased to 634 ± 22 µF for GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs. The increased capacitance showed that the N-rGONs create more electrocatalytic active surface area and enhance the electron transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalysis of d-glucose at modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic activity of the GCE|NiPcNRs, GCE|N-rGONs, and GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs modified electrodes towards direct glucose oxidation was investigated using cyclic voltammetry. Fig. 7 presents the CVs of (a) GCE, (b) GCE|NiPcNRs, (c) GCE|N-rGONs, and (d) GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs in the (i) absence and (ii) 2.0 mM d-glucose in 0.50 M KOH at a scan rate of 50 mV.s−1. Fig. 7(a) and 7(b) showed a small current response on the bare GCE and GCE|N-rGONs in the absence and in 2.0 mM d-glucose, confirming that the bare GCE and N-rGONs slowly catalyzed d-glucose electrooxidation. The CVs of GCE|NiPcNRs, and GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs in Fig. 7(c) and 7(d) showed an increase in the anodic and a decrease in the cathodic current density in the presence of d-glucose. The current response demonstrated the electrocatalysis of GCE|NiPcNRs and GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs towards glucose oxidation. The glucose oxidation potential coincides with the NiII/NiIII redox peaks, which shows that the d-glucose oxidation occurs at the highly catalytic NiIII redox-active sites. The glucose oxidation onset potential was 0.53 V (vs Ag|AgCl) for the GCE|NiPcNRs and 0.45 V (vs Ag|AgCl) for GCE|N-rGON1|NiPc5NRs. The lowered onset glucose oxidation potential and higher current density of the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs compared with the GCE|NiPcNRs could be attributed to an increase in the electroactive surface area and the synergistic excellent electron transfer effect of the conductive N-rGONs.

The effect of NiPcNRs incorporated with the N-rGONs on d-glucose electrooxidation was investigated. Fig. S3 shows the CVs of (a) GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc0.5NRs, (b) GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc1NRs, (c) GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc2NRs and (d) GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc10NRs in 0.50 M KOH containing 2.0 mM d-glucose. The anodic current density increased with the ratio of N-rGONs to NiPcNRs and was optimum at the N-rGONs|NiPcNRs ratio of ≥ 5.0. The changes in the current density magnitude (ΔI) for 2.0 mmol.L-1 was 70.7 µA.cm−2 at GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc0.5NRs, 1.41 mA.cm −2 for GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc1NRs, 2.97 mA.cm −2 for GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc2NRs, 14.1 mA.cm−2 for GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs and 11.3 mA.cm−2 for GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc10NRs. The GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs had the optimum high catalytic response and was used for further studies.

The influence of KOH concentrations on d-glucose electrooxidation was studied. The CVs of GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs towards 2.0 mM glucose were recorded in different concentrations of KOH. Fig. 8 shows CVs of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs (a) in different KOH concentrations (0.10 to 2.0 M) (b) at different scan rates in 0.50 M KOH with 1.0 mM d-glucose. Fig. 8 (a') and 8(b') show the corresponding plot of current density magnitude against KOH concentrations and scan rate, respectively. The anodic and cathodic peak potentials shift to lower values with increasing KOH concentrations from 0.10 M to 2.0 M. The anodic current density response was at maxima at 0.50 M KOH. Hence, 0.50 M KOH was the optimum KOH concentration for further studies. The electrokinetic of glucose oxidation was further investigated by changing the CV scan rate. Fig. 8(b) shows the CVs of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs in 0.50 M KOH solution containing 1.0 mM glucose at different scan rates. The plot of the anodic peak and cathodic peak current against the square root of the scan rate in Fig. 8 (b') was observed to be linear. The linear regression equations y = 0.082 √ v + 1.98 (R2 = 0.99) and y = − 0.56 √ v + 0.083 (R2 = 0.99) were obtained for the anodic and cathodic peaks, respectively. The linearity showed that glucose oxidation at the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs is a diffusion-controlled process. The rate constant ( α ) of the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs electrode reaction was calculated from Eq. (1) [58] .

where α is the electron transfer coefficient, and Sc and Sa are the slopes of the cathodic and anodic peaks, respectively. The electron transfer coefficient for the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs was calculated and found to be 0.58, which is close to 0.50 for a typical irreversible process [59]. The glucose oxidation at the GCE|N-rGONs|NiPcNRs is irreversible.

Equations (2) - (5) describe the mechanism of the d-glucose oxidation at the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs electrode. The NiIIPcNRs species of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs in an alkaline electrolyte solution are electrochemically oxidized to highly reactive NiIII(OH)PcNRs oxyhydroxide species in Eq.
(2) . The NiIII(OH)PcNRs species are strong oxidants with a suitable d-orbital to coordinate d-glucose molecules in Eq.
(3) rapidly. Then, glucose undergoes hydrogen abstraction at the N-rGONs|NiIII(OH)PcNRs surface to form glucose-radical intermediates and regenerate the NiIIPcNRs species in Eq.
(4) . The hydroxyl ions in the electrolytes rapidly convert the d-glucose radical intermediates to gluconolactone, which releases electrons resulting in a peak current in Eq.
(5) . The N-rGONs unit of the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs facilitates faster electron transfer during this process. The proposed redox mechanism is similar to Ni-based electrochemical d-glucose sensors [23,29] .

The analytical performance of the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs was evaluated using cyclic voltammetry and amperometry methods to analyze standard glucose solutions. Fig. 9 shows (a) CVs of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs in different d-glucose concentrations at a scan rate of 50 mV.s−1, and (b) a corresponding calibration plot of current against d-glucose concentrations. The CVs in Fig. 9(a) showed an increased anodic current and a decreased cathodic current with increasing glucose concentrations. The plot of anodic current against glucose concentrations yielded an analytical curve with a linear dynamic range (LDR) between 0.25 and 20.0 mM, covering the glucose diagnostic range. The linear regression equation is expressed as Ipa (mA) = 103.1 [glucose] (mM) + 153.3, (R2 = 0.99). The sensor could detect glucose in patients with normal levels (≤7.8 mM), prediabetes (7.8 – 11.1 mM), and diabetes (≥11.1 mM). The sensitivity of 1458 µA.mM−1.cm−2 was obtained for the cyclic voltammetry detection of glucose. The limit of detection (LoD) for the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs was 1.34 µM of glucose. The LoD was calculated from the lowest detectable concentration of glucose.

Amperometric sensors are more desirable for continuous d-glucose monitoring than voltammetric sensors. The amperometric measurements were conducted at an applied potential (Eapp) of 0.58 V (vs Ag|AgCl). The Eapp was selected based on the information from the CVs Fig. 9(c) shows the current–time response of successive injections of different concentrations of d-glucose from 5.0 µM to 6.0 mM in 0.50 M KOH solution under continuous stirring. A rapid current increase was observed for every addition of glucose solution. The current reached a steady state within 0.8 s, indicating a rapid amperometric response. The calibration plot of the current peak height against the d-glucose concentrations in Fig. 9(d) showed a linear dynamic range from 5.0 µM to 6.0 mM (R2 = 0.989). At a concentration above 10.0 mM, the deviation from linearity was observed due to saturation of the active sites. The sensitivity of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs was calculated to be 358.9 µA.mM−1.cm−2 for the glucose concentration. The amperometric measurement had a lower sensitivity and higher LoD (5.0 µM) than the cyclic voltammetric method due to the application of a fixed potential (Eapp = 0.58 V). The fixed potential prevents the development of background charging currents, observed in the voltammetric method, due to changing potential with time. The lower LoD of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs would allow for its practical application to detect d-glucose concentrations in diluted serum samples.

The practical relevance of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs was tested to detect d-glucose concentrations in serum samples. Commercial fetal bovine serum (FBS) was spiked with d-glucose to a final concentration of 5.0 μM, 50.0 μM, 0.10 mM, and 0.20 mM. The glucose concentrations in the FBS and glucose-spiked FBS were analyzed using chronoamperometric techniques. The quantification of the glucose was determined from the linear plot in Fig. 9(d). Standard addition and recovery methods were used to validate the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs sensor response. The percentage recovery ranges from 99.5 % to 105.1 %. The intra-assay experiment %RSD ranged from 3.4 % to 5.2 %. The result showed that the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs have great d-glucose detection potential in a complex serum matrix. Table 1 compares the analytical performance of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs with other nickel electrocatalyst-based d-glucose sensors. The GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs sensor has a lower detection limit, wider linear range, and faster response time than other reported nickel-based d-glucose sensors. The excellent analytical performance of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs was attributed to:
(i)
effective formation of highly catalytic NiIII(OH)Pc5NRs active species in alkaline solution,
(ii)
highly conductive N-rGONs facilitate efficient transfer electrons process,
(iii)
3D structure of N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs provides a large surface area for adsorption of glucose molecule onto the electrode surface, and
(iv)
synergistic effect of high catalytic activity of NiPcNR and conductivity of N-rGONs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference, stability, and reproducibility of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs
ce:section.ce:para: The significant limitation of the non-enzymatic d-glucose sensor is the signal interference caused by the oxidation of coexisting endogenous electroactive biomolecules in human blood. The selectivity of the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs for d-glucose in the presence of endogenous electroactive substances such as uric acid (UA), dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), sucrose (Suc), fructose (Fru), and K+, Na+, Cl- ions was evaluated. Fig. 10 (a) shows the amperometric response of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs to 0.25 mM of glucose, 0.10 M NaCl, and 0.50 mM of other interfering substances in 0.50 mol.L-1 KOH. The addition of Suc, UA, DA, KCl, Fru, and AA did not produce a significant current response. The GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs has similar current response before and after the addition of the interfering substances, indicating the high anti-interference capability of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs and good selectivity for d-glucose. The excellent selectivity of GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs implies its potential practical application for d-glucose detection in serum samples. The stability and reproducibility of the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs sensor were also evaluated. The amperometric response to the repeated addition of 0.25 mM of d-glucose at 30 s interval in Fig. 10(b) showed a highly reproducible current response. The %RSD was < 5 % confirming excellent sensor repeatability. The long-term storage stability and reusability of the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs were investigated by storing the sensor at room temperature. The current response to 0.50 mM d-glucose in 0.50 M KOH was measured every-five days for 40 days. Fig. 10(c) shows that the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs sensor is highly stable, retaining about 94.3 % of the initial sensor response after 40 days. The percentage relative standard deviation (%RSD) for the repetitive measurements within the 40 days was 3.38 %, demonstrating excellent stability and reusability of the GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs sensor. The excellent storage stability and reusability of the sensor are due to the air, thermal, and chemical stability of the NiPcNRs and N-rGONs as a nanocomposite material.

We have demonstrated a facile synthesis of nickel phthalocyanine nanorods and its nanocomposite with nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide nanosheets (N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs) as an electrocatalytic material. The d-glucose sensing properties of the N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs modified glassy carbon electrode was systematically investigated. A highly stable, sensitive, and selective non-enzymatic d-glucose sensor has been developed. The as-prepared GCE|N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs sensors showed good electrocatalytic activity towards glucose oxidation and demonstrated a detection limit in the micromolar range. More importantly, the N-rGONs1|NiPc5NRs achieved highly accurate, selective, and reliable detection of low glucose levels in serum, demonstrating its great potential in achieving simple, low-cost, rapid, and sensitive point-of-care glucose monitoring.
